walkthrough.title: Player Decks
walkthrough.text: Let's get Marvel Legendary set up. First, each player builds their own personal starting deck. Every player gets the same thing: 8 S.H.I.E.L.D. Agents and 4 S.H.I.E.L.D. Troopers, for a total of 12 cards. S.H.I.E.L.D. Agents give you 1 Recruit Point each, and S.H.I.E.L.D. Troopers give you 1 Attack each. Shuffle your 12-card deck and place it face down in front of you. These are your basic heroes — they are not flashy, but they will fund your first few recruits.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Game Stacks
walkthrough.text: Now place three shared supply stacks where everyone can reach them. First, the S.H.I.E.L.D. Officer stack — there are 30 of these. S.H.I.E.L.D. Officers are basic heroes anyone can recruit at any time for 3 Recruit Points. Second, the Wound stack — 30 Wound cards. You will not want these, but various villain abilities will force them into your deck. Third, the Bystander stack — 30 Bystander cards. These represent innocent civilians who get caught up in the chaos. They will be shuffled into the Villain Deck and captured by villains during the game.

walkthrough.title: Choose the Mastermind
walkthrough.text: Now pick which Mastermind you will face. You can choose one or draw randomly. The Mastermind is the big boss villain that all players are working together to defeat. Place the Mastermind card face up in the upper-left area of the play space. Each Mastermind has an Attack value you will need to match, a Master Strike effect that punishes all players, and a special ability that says which Villain Group the Mastermind 'Always Leads.' Tuck the Mastermind's four Mastermind Tactic cards face down underneath it — these have the same Attack value but different Fight effects.

walkthrough.title: Choose the Scheme
walkthrough.text: Next, pick a Scheme card — again, randomly or by choice. The Scheme is the Mastermind's evil plan, and it defines how the villains can win. Place it face up next to the Mastermind. Read the Scheme carefully. Every Scheme has a Setup section that tells you how many Scheme Twist cards go into the Villain Deck. Many Schemes also have unique special rules that change the game. The 'Evil Wins' condition on the Scheme tells you exactly how the players can lose.

walkthrough.title: Build the Villain Deck — Villain Groups
walkthrough.text: Now we build the Villain Deck, which is the engine that drives the game's threat. Start with the Villain Groups. The Mastermind's card says it 'Always Leads' a specific Villain Group — that group is always included. Each Villain Group has 8 cards. Then add additional Villain Groups based on player count: 2 players use 2 Villain Groups total, 3 players use 3, 4 players use 3, and 5 players use 4. Extra groups beyond the Mastermind's required group are chosen randomly.

walkthrough.title: Build the Villain Deck — Henchman Groups
walkthrough.text: Next, add Henchman Groups to the Villain Deck. Henchmen are weaker villains — each Henchman Group has 10 identical cards. The number of Henchman Groups depends on player count: 2 players use 1, 3 players use 1, 4 players use 2, and 5 players use 2. Choose Henchman Groups randomly.

walkthrough.title: Build the Villain Deck — Bystanders, Master Strikes, and Scheme Twists
walkthrough.text: Now add the remaining cards to the Villain Deck. Always add exactly 5 Master Strike cards — these trigger the Mastermind's Master Strike ability. Add Bystander cards from the Bystander stack based on player count: 2 players get 2, 3 players get 8, 4 players get 8, and 5 players get 12. Finally, add the number of Scheme Twist cards specified on the Scheme card's Setup section. Shuffle the entire Villain Deck thoroughly and place it face down to the right of the city spaces.

walkthrough.title: Build the Hero Deck
walkthrough.text: There are 15 different Heroes in the game, each with 14 cards. For a game, you will only use a subset. Choose 5 Heroes randomly — that gives you 70 cards total for the Hero Deck. If you are playing with 5 players, add a sixth Hero for 84 cards. Shuffle all the selected Hero cards together into one face-down Hero Deck and place it to the right of the HQ spaces.

walkthrough.title: Fill the HQ
walkthrough.text: The HQ (Headquarters) is where heroes wait to be recruited. It has 5 face-up hero card spaces. Flip the top 5 cards from the Hero Deck and place them face up in the HQ spaces. These are the heroes available for recruitment on the first turn. Whenever a hero is recruited from the HQ, the empty space is immediately refilled from the Hero Deck.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands and Begin
walkthrough.text: Each player draws a hand of 6 cards from their personal deck. Choose a starting player randomly. Play proceeds clockwise. The Villain Deck is ready, the HQ is stocked with heroes, and the Mastermind is scheming. Time to save the world. Let's learn the rules.

bullets: Each player gets 8 S.H.I.E.L.D. Agents (1 Recruit each) + 4 S.H.I.E.L.D. Troopers (1 Attack each)
Shuffle your 12-card deck and place it face down

bullets: S.H.I.E.L.D. Officer stack: 30 cards (recruitable for 3 Recruit any time)
Wound stack: 30 cards (gained from villain abilities)
Bystander stack: 30 cards (innocent civilians shuffled into Villain Deck)

summary.title: Choose the Mastermind
bullets: Place Mastermind card face up with 4 Mastermind Tactic cards face down underneath
Check which Villain Group the Mastermind 'Always Leads'
Note the Master Strike effect and Attack value

summary.title: Choose the Scheme
bullets: Place Scheme card face up next to the Mastermind
Read the Setup section for Scheme Twist count
Note any special rules and the 'Evil Wins' condition

summary.title: Build the Villain Deck — Villain Groups
bullets: Always include the Mastermind's 'Always Leads' Villain Group (8 cards each)
2 players: 2 groups total | 3 players: 3 | 4 players: 3 | 5 players: 4
Extra groups chosen randomly

2 players: 1 group | 3 players: 1 | 4 players: 2 | 5 players: 2

bullets: Always add 5 Master Strike cards
Bystanders: 2 players: 2 | 3 players: 8 | 4 players: 8 | 5 players: 12

Shuffle entire Villain Deck and place face down

Shuffle into one Hero Deck, place face down

bullets: HQ has 5 face-up spaces for hero cards
Flip top 5 Hero Deck cards into HQ
Refill immediately whenever a hero is recruited

summary.title: Deal Starting Hands and Begin
bullets: Each player draws 6 cards from their personal deck
Choose a starting player randomly, play clockwise

walkthrough.title: How to Win (and How to Lose)
walkthrough.text: Marvel Legendary is a cooperative deck-building game where players work together to defeat the Mastermind. Players win by defeating the Mastermind 4 times — each time you fight the Mastermind, you claim one of their 4 Mastermind Tactic cards. Once all 4 Tactics are gone, the Mastermind is defeated and the players win. But the game fights back. The Mastermind has an evil Scheme, and if the Scheme's 'Evil Wins' condition is met, all players lose immediately. Even if the players win together, the player with the most Victory Points is the ultimate champion — so there is both a cooperative and competitive element.

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview — Three Steps
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you follow three steps in order. Step 1: Flip the top card of the Villain Deck — this is mandatory and happens before you do anything else. Step 2: Play cards from your hand to generate Recruit Points and Attack, then use those to recruit heroes and fight villains. Step 3: End your turn by discarding all played cards and your remaining hand, then draw 6 new cards. Let's walk through each step in detail.

walkthrough.title: Step 1 — Villain Deck Card: Villain Enters the City
walkthrough.text: At the start of your turn, flip the top card of the Villain Deck. There are four types of cards that can come up. If it is a Villain, that villain enters the city. The city has 5 spaces in a row. New villains always enter the space closest to the Villain Deck, then push existing villains one space forward along the track. If a villain gets pushed off the 5th and final space, it escapes. When a villain escapes: it KOs a hero that costs 6 or less from the HQ (you choose which), any captured Bystanders it carried are lost (each player discards a card as a penalty), and any Escape effect on the villain triggers. Villains in the city are always face up.

walkthrough.title: Step 1 — Villain Deck Card: Other Card Types
walkthrough.text: If the Villain Deck card is a Bystander, the Bystander is captured by the villain in the city closest to the Villain Deck. If there are no villains, the Mastermind captures the Bystander. Captured Bystanders move with their villain. If you defeat that villain, you rescue the Bystander into your Victory Pile, worth 1 Victory Point each. If the card is a Scheme Twist, the Twist effect on the Scheme card triggers immediately, advancing the Mastermind's evil plan. The Scheme Twist is then removed from play unless the Scheme says otherwise. If the card is a Master Strike, the Mastermind's Master Strike effect on the Mastermind card triggers, punishing all players. The Master Strike card is then removed from play. Important: Bystanders, Scheme Twists, and Master Strikes do NOT push villains forward in the city.

walkthrough.title: Step 1 — Ambush and Escape Effects
walkthrough.text: Some villain cards have special Ambush or Escape keywords. An Ambush effect triggers the moment a new villain enters the city. An Escape effect triggers when a villain is pushed off the end of the city. If a new villain entering causes an existing villain to escape, resolve all Escape effects for the escaping villain first, then resolve the new villain's Ambush effect. These effects can chain — a single villain entering may cause multiple villains to shift and one to escape.

walkthrough.title: Step 2 — Playing Cards from Your Hand
walkthrough.text: After the Villain Deck card resolves, you play cards from your hand one at a time in any order. Each hero card has Recruit Points (star icon) and Attack (claw/fist icon) printed on it, plus potentially a special ability. As you play cards, you accumulate Recruit Points and Attack for the turn. Some cards have a number with a '+' symbol, meaning you always get at least that amount but might get more based on the card's special ability. The cards you play stay on the table in front of you until the end of your turn.

walkthrough.title: Step 2 — Combo Powers (Hero Classes)
walkthrough.text: Some hero cards have a Combo Power — a special ability with a hero class icon and a colon, like a Strength icon followed by 'You get +2 Attack.' You can only use that Combo Power if you have already played another card of the same hero class earlier this turn. A card's hero class is shown by the icon in its upper left corner and the color of its border. There are five hero classes: Strength, Intelligence, Skill, Magic, and Ranged. You can only use a card's Combo Power once per turn, even if you played multiple matching-class cards before it. Building a deck around one or two hero classes makes Combo Powers fire much more reliably.

walkthrough.title: Step 2 — Recruiting Heroes
walkthrough.text: Use your accumulated Recruit Points to gain heroes from the HQ, one at a time. Each hero card in the HQ has a cost in the lower-right corner. Spend Recruit Points equal to that cost and place the hero into your discard pile. When your deck runs out and your discard pile is shuffled into a new deck, you will draw that hero in the future. You can also always recruit S.H.I.E.L.D. Officers from the Officer stack for 3 Recruit Points each. Whenever an HQ space empties, it is immediately refilled from the Hero Deck. You can even recruit a hero, see what replaces it, and recruit that new hero too if you have enough Recruit Points.

walkthrough.title: Step 2 — Fighting Villains
walkthrough.text: Use your accumulated Attack to fight villains in the city, one at a time. You must have Attack equal to or greater than the villain's Attack value to defeat it. The defeated villain and any captured Bystanders go into your personal Victory Pile. If the villain has a Fight effect, it triggers when you defeat it. You keep any leftover Attack to fight additional villains or even the Mastermind. You can recruit heroes and fight villains in any order during your turn, and you can mix recruiting and fighting freely.

walkthrough.title: Step 2 — Fighting the Mastermind
walkthrough.text: You can choose to fight the Mastermind instead of or in addition to fighting villains. You need Attack equal to the Mastermind's Attack value. When you fight the Mastermind, draw a random Mastermind Tactic card from those tucked underneath. That Tactic goes into your Victory Pile — it is worth Victory Points. Then the Fight effect on that Tactic triggers. The Mastermind is not truly defeated until all 4 Tactic cards have been claimed by players. If you generate enough Attack, you can even fight the Mastermind multiple times in one turn. Defeating any Mastermind Tactic also rescues any Bystanders captured by the Mastermind. Important: Masterminds are not 'Villains' for card abilities — effects that target Villains do not affect Masterminds.

walkthrough.title: Step 3 — End Your Turn
walkthrough.text: When you are done recruiting and fighting, end your turn. All cards you played this turn and any remaining cards in your hand go into your discard pile. Then draw 6 new cards from your deck. If your deck runs out mid-draw, shuffle your discard pile to form a new deck and continue drawing. Your discard pile is only shuffled when your deck is completely empty and you still need to draw or reveal cards. Play passes to the next player clockwise.

walkthrough.title: KO, Wounds, and Healing
walkthrough.text: Many abilities say to 'KO' cards — this means the card is permanently removed from the game into the shared KO pile. KO-ing your weak starting heroes is actually good — it means you draw your powerful recruited heroes more often. Wounds are bad cards forced into your deck by villain abilities. They have no Recruit or Attack, so drawing Wounds weakens your hand. Wound cards have a Healing ability: if you do not recruit any heroes or defeat any villains on your turn, you may KO all Wounds from your hand. Sometimes it is worth spending a turn just to heal. Some hero cards also have abilities that let you KO Wounds.

walkthrough.title: Victory Pile and Scoring
walkthrough.text: Each player has a personal Victory Pile that holds defeated villains and rescued Bystanders. Villains and Bystanders are never shuffled into your deck. At the end of the game, if the players defeated the Mastermind, each player adds up the Victory Points shown on the cards in their Victory Pile. Mastermind Tactic cards are typically worth 5 Victory Points each. Bystanders are worth 1 Victory Point each. Different villains are worth different amounts. The player with the most Victory Points is the individual winner and the most legendary hero.

walkthrough.title: Draw and Special Endings
walkthrough.text: If either the Hero Deck or the Villain Deck runs out of cards before the Scheme is completed, the game is a draw — the players survived but did not defeat the Mastermind. The player with the most Victory Points still wins an individual victory. If the S.H.I.E.L.D. Officer, Wound, or Bystander stacks run out, the game continues normally — if a card would be gained from an empty stack, it simply is not gained. Rescue a Bystander abilities let you take the top Bystander from the stack into your Victory Pile — but they do not let you rescue Bystanders captured by specific villains in the city. You must defeat those villains to save their captives.

summary.title: How to Win (and How to Lose)
bullets: Players cooperate to defeat the Mastermind 4 times (claim all 4 Tactic cards)
If the Scheme's 'Evil Wins' condition triggers, all players lose
If players win, highest Victory Points = individual champion

Step 2: Play cards, recruit heroes, fight villains
Step 3: Discard everything, draw 6 new cards

These three card types do NOT push villains forward

Resolve Escape effects before Ambush effects

Five classes: Strength, Intelligence, Skill, Magic, Ranged
Each Combo Power triggers at most once per turn
Focus your deck on 1-2 classes for reliable combos

KO-ing starting heroes thins your deck (good!)

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with three players — Player 1, Player 2, and Player 3. Each player starts with the same 12-card deck: 8 S.H.I.E.L.D. Agents (1 Recruit each) and 4 S.H.I.E.L.D. Troopers (1 Attack each). Everyone has drawn their starting hand of 6 cards. The HQ has 5 heroes face up, the Villain Deck is shuffled and ready, and the Mastermind is waiting. Let's play a few turns.

walkthrough.title: The Board at Start
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what we are working with. The Mastermind is face up with an Attack of 8 and 4 Tactic cards tucked underneath. The Scheme is in play with its Twist condition visible. The city is completely empty — no villains yet. The HQ has 5 hero cards face up with costs ranging from 2 to 7. The S.H.I.E.L.D. Officer stack is ready at cost 3. Each player has 6 cards in hand from their starting deck.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn — Step 1: Villain Deck
walkthrough.text: Player 1, you are up first. Before you do anything, flip the top card of the Villain Deck. It is a Villain with an Attack of 3. This villain enters the city in the space closest to the Villain Deck. The city was empty, so no other villains get pushed. The villain is face up in the first city space. Note any Ambush text — if it says Ambush, that effect triggers right now.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn — Step 2: Play Cards
walkthrough.text: Now play the 6 cards in your hand. Player 1 drew 4 S.H.I.E.L.D. Agents and 2 S.H.I.E.L.D. Troopers. Play them all. That gives you 4 Recruit Points and 2 Attack. You have enough Attack to fight the villain in the city (Attack 3) — wait, you only have 2 Attack. You cannot fight it. But you have 4 Recruit Points. Look at the HQ. There is a hero that costs 3 and another that costs 4. You recruit the cost-3 hero — it goes into your discard pile. You have 1 Recruit Point left, not enough for anything else in the HQ or even a S.H.I.E.L.D. Officer (cost 3).

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Turn — Step 3: End Turn
walkthrough.text: Player 1 discards all played cards and any remaining hand cards into the discard pile. Then draws 6 new cards from the deck. The HQ refills the empty space immediately with a new hero from the Hero Deck. Good first turn — you got a hero that will show up in future draws. Play passes to Player 2.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn — Step 1: Villain Deck
walkthrough.text: Player 2, flip the top card of the Villain Deck. This time it is a Bystander. The Bystander gets captured by the villain in the city closest to the Villain Deck — that is the villain Player 1 saw enter. Tuck the Bystander card under that villain. If Player 2 or anyone defeats that villain later, they rescue the Bystander for 1 Victory Point. Important: Bystanders do NOT push villains forward.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Turn — Step 2: Play Cards and Recruit
walkthrough.text: Player 2 drew 3 S.H.I.E.L.D. Agents and 3 S.H.I.E.L.D. Troopers — that is 3 Recruit and 3 Attack. The villain in the city has Attack 3, and you have exactly 3 Attack. You could fight it and rescue the Bystander. But Player 2 decides to recruit a cost-3 hero from the HQ instead, spending all 3 Recruit. The 3 Attack goes unused. The recruited hero goes into the discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake — Wasting Attack
walkthrough.text: That was a mistake. Player 2 had 3 Attack — exactly enough to defeat the villain in the city and rescue the Bystander (worth 1 VP for the Bystander plus the villain's own VP value). Instead, all that Attack was completely wasted because it was not spent. Attack does not carry over between turns. Player 2 could have fought the villain AND recruited a hero in the same turn since recruiting and fighting can happen in any order. The lesson: always look for villains you can defeat before ending your turn. Attack that goes unspent is Attack thrown away.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn — Step 1: Villain Deck
walkthrough.text: Player 3, flip the Villain Deck. This time a second Villain enters the city with Attack 5. It enters the first city space, pushing the existing villain (the one with the captured Bystander) one space forward. Now there are 2 villains in the city. If the new villain has an Ambush effect, it triggers now — but only after any Escape effects from pushed villains. In this case, nobody escaped because there was room.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Turn — Step 2: Play and Fight
walkthrough.text: Player 3 drew 5 S.H.I.E.L.D. Agents and 1 S.H.I.E.L.D. Trooper — that is 5 Recruit and 1 Attack. Not enough Attack to fight either villain (they need 3 and 5 respectively). With 5 Recruit, Player 3 recruits a powerful hero costing 5 from the HQ. A great pickup that will generate strong Recruit or Attack in future turns. The HQ refills immediately.

walkthrough.title: Player 1's Second Turn — Scheme Twist!
walkthrough.text: Back to Player 1 for their second turn. Flip the Villain Deck — it is a Scheme Twist! The Twist effect on the Scheme card triggers immediately. This might force all players to discard cards, gain Wounds, or advance the evil win condition. Read the Scheme to see what happens. After resolving the Twist, Player 1 continues with Step 2 as normal. Scheme Twists do NOT push villains forward.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 — Recruiting with a Plan
walkthrough.text: Player 1 drew their newly recruited hero from last turn along with starting cards. The new hero gives 2 Attack plus a Combo Power: if you played another card of the same hero class this turn, gain +2 Attack. Unfortunately, Player 1 does not have another card of that class yet — the starting S.H.I.E.L.D. cards are basic grey heroes, not a named class. The Combo Power does not fire this time. Player 1 should look to recruit more heroes of the same class to unlock Combo Powers in future turns. For now, Player 1 recruits another hero and ends the turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Second Turn — Another Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player 2 flips a villain card — a third villain enters the city, pushing everyone forward. Things are getting crowded. Player 2 plays their hand and gets 4 Recruit and 2 Attack. Not enough Attack to fight any villain in the city. Player 2 panics and recruits two S.H.I.E.L.D. Officers for 3 Recruit each — wait. Player 2 only has 4 Recruit total, not 6. They cannot recruit two Officers. This is the second mistake: miscounting Recruit Points. Always add up your Recruit and Attack totals carefully before spending them.

walkthrough.title: Correcting Player 2
walkthrough.text: Let's fix that. Player 2 has 4 Recruit Points, not 6. They can recruit one S.H.I.E.L.D. Officer for 3, leaving 1 Recruit unspent. Or they could recruit a hero from the HQ that costs exactly 4. Looking at the HQ, there is a cost-4 hero — much better value than an Officer. Player 2 recruits it. Lesson: always scan the HQ before defaulting to S.H.I.E.L.D. Officers. Officers are always available, but HQ heroes are usually more powerful for the same or similar cost.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Second Turn — Master Strike!
walkthrough.text: Player 3 flips the Villain Deck and reveals a Master Strike. The Mastermind's Master Strike ability triggers — for example, 'Each player reveals a Hero card or discards down to four cards.' Every player must resolve this effect. Master Strikes can be devastating, especially when players have weak hands. After resolving, Player 3 continues their turn normally. There are 5 Master Strikes in the Villain Deck, and each one hurts.

walkthrough.title: Player 3 — Fighting the First Villain
walkthrough.text: Player 3 drew their cost-5 hero from earlier plus starting cards. Between the recruited hero and the S.H.I.E.L.D. Troopers, Player 3 has 4 Attack. The first villain in the city (the one pushed farthest forward with the captured Bystander) has Attack 3. Player 3 has enough! They defeat the villain. The villain card and the Bystander both go into Player 3's Victory Pile. The villain's Fight effect triggers if it has one. Player 3 has 1 Attack remaining — not enough to fight the other villains, but a solid turn.

walkthrough.title: Why Player 3 Chose Wisely
walkthrough.text: Player 3 made a smart choice fighting the villain with the Bystander. That earns Victory Points from both the villain and the rescued Bystander. They also cleared a city space, which means villains have more room before escaping. When deciding which villain to fight, always consider: the VP value, any captured Bystanders, whether the villain is close to escaping, and whether the Fight effect helps or hurts you.

walkthrough.title: Building Toward Combo Powers
walkthrough.text: After a few turns, players start drawing their recruited heroes alongside starting cards. This is when Combo Powers begin firing. If Player 1 recruited two Intelligence-class heroes and draws them in the same hand, the second one's Combo Power activates because the first one was already played. Suddenly you are generating 5 or 6 Attack from a single hand instead of 2 or 3. This is the deck-building payoff — your deck gets stronger and stronger as you add powerful heroes and thin out weak starting cards.

walkthrough.title: Player 2's Mistake — Ignoring the City
walkthrough.text: A few more turns pass. The city now has 4 villains, with the farthest one about to escape. Player 2 has 5 Attack this turn — enough to defeat the villain about to escape (Attack 4). But Player 2 looks at the HQ and sees an amazing 7-cost hero they really want. They have exactly 7 Recruit. Tempted, Player 2 recruits the hero and ignores the villain. Next turn, a new villain enters the city, and the Attack-4 villain escapes — KO-ing a hero from the HQ and forcing every player to discard a card. Player 2's greed hurt the entire team.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Team Responsibility
walkthrough.text: Marvel Legendary is cooperative first, competitive second. When a villain is about to escape, the team needs someone to fight it. Letting a villain escape costs the team a hero from the HQ, discards from everyone's hand, and potentially triggers a nasty Escape effect. Recruiting a great hero feels good for you, but a villain escaping feels bad for everyone. Prioritize fighting villains that are about to escape, especially ones carrying Bystanders.

walkthrough.title: The Mastermind Fight
walkthrough.text: Now let's fast-forward to a pivotal moment. Player 3 has built a strong deck with several heroes of the same class. They draw an amazing hand: two Intelligence heroes that chain Combo Powers, plus a hero that gives +3 Attack. Total: 9 Attack. The Mastermind has Attack 8. Player 3 has enough! They fight the Mastermind, drawing a random Tactic card. The Tactic goes into Player 3's Victory Pile (worth 5 VP), and the Tactic's Fight effect resolves. Three more Tactics to go before the players win!

walkthrough.title: The Race to Win
walkthrough.text: The tension builds as Scheme Twists keep coming. Each one brings the Mastermind closer to victory. The players need to defeat the Mastermind 3 more times. This is the dual clock of Marvel Legendary — the players are racing to defeat the Mastermind while the Scheme races toward 'Evil Wins.' Build your deck fast, fight villains to keep the city clear, and generate enough Attack to challenge the Mastermind whenever possible.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now seen all the core mechanics in action: flipping Villain Deck cards, recruiting heroes, fighting villains, Combo Powers, fighting the Mastermind, Scheme Twists, Master Strikes, Bystander rescues, and city management. You watched Player 2 learn from three common mistakes — wasting Attack, miscounting Recruit, and ignoring escaping villains. You saw Player 3 build a focused deck and take down the Mastermind. You are ready to play a full game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Battle
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics on deck-building and when to fight versus recruit.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every keyword, hero class, villain type, and game term.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun saving the world!

summary.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 3 players demonstrating a first round
Each player starts with 8 S.H.I.E.L.D. Agents + 4 S.H.I.E.L.D. Troopers
Starting hand of 6 cards each

summary.title: The Board at Start
bullets: Mastermind face up with Attack 8 and 4 Tactics underneath
City empty, HQ has 5 face-up heroes, Villain Deck ready
S.H.I.E.L.D. Officer stack available at cost 3

summary.title: Player 1's Turn — Step 1: Villain Deck
bullets: Flip top Villain Deck card — a Villain with Attack 3
Villain enters city in the first space
Check for Ambush effects on the villain card

summary.title: Player 1's Turn — Step 2: Play Cards
bullets: 4 Agents + 2 Troopers = 4 Recruit, 2 Attack
Not enough Attack to fight the villain (needs 3)
Recruits a cost-3 hero from the HQ

summary.title: Player 1's Turn — Step 3: End Turn
bullets: Discard all cards, draw 6 new from deck
HQ refills the empty space immediately
Good first turn — invested in a better hero

summary.title: Player 2's Turn — Bystander Captured
bullets: Villain Deck reveals a Bystander
Bystander captured by the villain closest to the Villain Deck
Bystanders do NOT push villains forward

summary.title: Player 2's Turn — Recruit Only
bullets: 3 Agents + 3 Troopers = 3 Recruit, 3 Attack
Could fight the villain (Attack 3) but chooses to recruit instead
3 Attack goes completely unused

summary.title: Player 2's Mistake — Wasting Attack
bullets: Attack does not carry over between turns — unused = wasted
Could have fought villain AND recruited in the same turn
Lesson: always check if you can defeat a villain before ending your turn

summary.title: Player 3's Turn — Second Villain Enters
bullets: New villain (Attack 5) enters the city, pushes existing villains forward
Now 2 villains in the city
Resolve Escape effects before Ambush effects

summary.title: Player 3's Turn — Recruit a Strong Hero
bullets: 5 Agents + 1 Trooper = 5 Recruit, 1 Attack
Not enough Attack to fight either villain
Recruits a cost-5 hero — strong future investment

summary.title: Player 1's Second Turn — Scheme Twist!
bullets: Villain Deck reveals a Scheme Twist
Twist effect on the Scheme triggers — may hurt all players
Scheme Twists do NOT push villains
Player 1 continues with Step 2 normally after resolving

summary.title: Player 1 — Combo Power Not Yet Active
bullets: Recruited hero has a Combo Power requiring a matching hero class
Starting S.H.I.E.L.D. cards are basic grey — don't match
Need to recruit more same-class heroes to unlock combos
Player 1 recruits another hero and ends turn

summary.title: Player 2's Mistake — Miscounting Recruit
bullets: Player 2 tries to recruit two S.H.I.E.L.D. Officers (cost 3 each = 6 total)
But only has 4 Recruit — cannot afford two Officers
Lesson: always count Recruit and Attack totals carefully before spending

summary.title: Correcting Player 2
bullets: 4 Recruit = 1 Officer (3) with 1 left over, or 1 HQ hero costing 4
HQ hero at cost 4 is better value than an Officer at cost 3
Lesson: scan the HQ before defaulting to S.H.I.E.L.D. Officers

summary.title: Player 3's Turn — Master Strike!
bullets: Villain Deck reveals a Master Strike
Mastermind's Master Strike ability triggers — affects all players
5 Master Strikes total in the Villain Deck
Player 3 continues their turn after resolving

summary.title: Player 3 — First Villain Defeated
bullets: Recruited hero + Troopers = 4 Attack, villain has Attack 3
Player 3 defeats villain and rescues the captured Bystander
Both go to Victory Pile; villain's Fight effect triggers

summary.title: Why Player 3 Chose Wisely
bullets: Fought villain with Bystander = VP from both
Cleared a city space, giving more room before escapes
Consider: VP value, Bystanders, escape risk, Fight effect

summary.title: Building Toward Combo Powers
bullets: Recruited heroes start appearing in drawn hands
Two same-class heroes in one hand triggers Combo Powers
Deck grows from 2-3 Attack per hand to 5-6+ with combos

summary.title: Player 2's Mistake — Ignoring the City
bullets: Player 2 has 5 Attack but recruits a hero instead of fighting an escaping villain
Villain escapes: KOs HQ hero, every player discards, Escape effect triggers
Player 2's greed hurt the entire team

summary.title: Lesson: Team Responsibility
bullets: Cooperative first, competitive second
Villain escaping hurts everyone — prioritize preventing escapes
Especially fight villains carrying Bystanders before they escape

summary.title: The Mastermind Fight
bullets: Player 3 chains Combo Powers for 9 Attack against Mastermind's 8
Draws a random Tactic card — goes to Victory Pile (5 VP)
Tactic's Fight effect triggers, 3 more Tactics remain

summary.title: The Race to Win
bullets: Dual clock: players racing to defeat Mastermind vs. Scheme racing to 'Evil Wins'
Build deck fast, keep city clear, generate Attack for Mastermind fights
Scheme Twists create urgency — cannot take too long

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Covered all core mechanics: Villain Deck, recruiting, fighting, Combo Powers, Mastermind
Player 2 learned from 3 mistakes: wasting Attack, miscounting, ignoring escapes
Player 3 built a focused deck and fought the Mastermind
You are ready for a full game

summary.title: Before You Battle
bullets: Check out General Tips for deck-building strategy
Reference the Appendix for keywords, hero classes, and game terms
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the most common beginner mistakes. First, wasting Attack — if you have enough Attack to defeat a villain, do it. Attack does not carry over between turns. Second, hoarding Recruit when there are villains about to escape the city. The team's survival comes before your personal deck improvement. Third, ignoring Combo Powers — a deck scattered across all five hero classes will rarely trigger combos. Fourth, forgetting to check the Scheme's 'Evil Wins' condition. If the Scheme says 'Evil Wins when 7 Scheme Twists occur,' you need to count how many have come up and plan accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Early Game — Recruit Aggressively
walkthrough.text: In the first few turns, your starting deck of S.H.I.E.L.D. Agents and Troopers will not generate enough Attack to fight most villains. That is expected. Focus on recruiting heroes from the HQ. Look for heroes that cost 2 to 4 Recruit — they are affordable with your starting cards and will start appearing in your hand quickly. Do not chase expensive heroes early. A cost-7 hero is powerful, but if you spend two turns saving up for it, you have wasted those turns. Two cost-3 heroes across two turns is better than one cost-7 hero.

walkthrough.title: Focus on One or Two Hero Classes
walkthrough.text: The most important deck-building principle in Marvel Legendary is hero class focus. Combo Powers only fire when you play two or more heroes of the same class in a single turn. If your deck has heroes from all five classes, the odds of drawing two matching heroes in a 6-card hand are low. If your deck has mostly Intelligence and Strength heroes, you will consistently trigger Combo Powers. When recruiting, look at the hero class icon in the upper left corner and prioritize classes you already have.

walkthrough.title: Thin Your Deck
walkthrough.text: Your starting S.H.I.E.L.D. Agents and Troopers are weak. Every time you draw one instead of a powerful recruited hero, your turn is less effective. Any card ability that lets you KO cards from your deck is extremely valuable. KO your starting heroes whenever you get the chance. A lean 8-card deck of powerful heroes draws incredible hands every time. Think of KO as pruning a garden — cutting away the weak cards lets the strong ones bloom.

walkthrough.title: Manage the City
walkthrough.text: The city has 5 spaces. When a villain escapes off the end, bad things happen: a hero gets KO'd from the HQ (reducing options for everyone), each player discards a card, and the villain's Escape effect triggers. With 3 or more players, villains enter the city faster than any single player can clear them. Coordinate with your team. If you see a villain about to escape on the next turn, fight it now — even if it means skipping a tempting recruit. The cost of a villain escaping almost always outweighs the benefit of recruiting one hero.

walkthrough.title: Balance Recruiting and Fighting
walkthrough.text: New players tend to fall into one of two traps. Some recruit constantly, building a dream deck while the city overflows with villains. Others fight everything in sight but never improve their deck, so their Attack never grows. The right balance depends on the game state. In early turns when the city is empty or has weak villains, recruit. When the city is filling up, fight. When a villain carries a Bystander, prioritize that fight for the extra Victory Points. Remember that you can always do both in the same turn — spending Recruit Points and Attack does not conflict.

walkthrough.title: S.H.I.E.L.D. Officers — When to Buy Them
walkthrough.text: S.H.I.E.L.D. Officers cost 3 Recruit and give 2 Recruit when played. They are always available, which makes them tempting as a default purchase. But resist buying too many. Officers are better than starting Agents (2 Recruit vs 1), but they do not have any hero class, so they never trigger Combo Powers. Buy an Officer when the HQ has nothing useful at your price point, or when you have 1-2 leftover Recruit after a good purchase. Do not fill your deck with Officers — you want named heroes with Combo Powers.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Scheme Clock
walkthrough.text: The Scheme is a ticking clock. Every Scheme Twist that comes up moves the evil plan forward. Some Schemes escalate — early Twists are mild, but Twists 5 through 8 can be devastating. Always know how many Scheme Twists have occurred and how many remain until 'Evil Wins.' If the Scheme is close to completing, shift your priorities entirely to defeating the Mastermind. A game where the players have amazing decks but lose to the Scheme is still a loss. Team survival always comes first.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't waste Attack — if you can defeat a villain, do it (Attack doesn't carry over)
Don't hoard Recruit when villains are about to escape the city
Avoid scattering your deck across all 5 hero classes — focus on 1-2
Always track the Scheme's 'Evil Wins' condition

summary.title: Early Game — Recruit Aggressively
bullets: Starting hands rarely generate enough Attack to fight villains
Recruit heroes costing 2-4 Recruit for fast deck improvement
Two cost-3 heroes > one cost-7 hero in the early game
Cheap heroes appear in your hand sooner

summary.title: Focus on One or Two Hero Classes
bullets: Combo Powers fire only with 2+ same-class heroes in one hand
Focused deck (1-2 classes) triggers combos consistently
Check hero class icon (upper left corner) when recruiting
Five classes: Strength, Intelligence, Skill, Magic, Ranged

summary.title: Thin Your Deck
bullets: KO starting S.H.I.E.L.D. Agents/Troopers whenever possible
Fewer weak cards = draw powerful heroes more often
Card abilities that KO from your deck are extremely valuable
A lean 8-card deck draws incredible hands every turn

summary.title: Manage the City
bullets: 5 city spaces — villain pushed off the end = escape
Escape: KOs a hero from HQ, each player discards, Escape effect triggers
Coordinate with team — fight villains about to escape
Escape cost almost always outweighs one recruiting opportunity

summary.title: Balance Recruiting and Fighting
bullets: Early turns with empty city: recruit to build deck
City filling up: shift to fighting
Villains with Bystanders = priority targets (extra VP)
You can recruit AND fight in the same turn

summary.title: S.H.I.E.L.D. Officers — When to Buy Them
bullets: Cost 3, give 2 Recruit — better than Agents but no hero class
Never trigger Combo Powers — don't fill your deck with them
Buy when HQ has nothing useful or with leftover Recruit
Named heroes with Combo Powers are almost always better

summary.title: Watch the Scheme Clock
bullets: Track how many Scheme Twists have occurred
Later Twists are often more devastating than early ones
If Scheme is near completion, prioritize fighting the Mastermind
Team survival (beating the Scheme) > individual VP

walkthrough.title: Deck Archetype — The Class Engine
walkthrough.text: The most reliable strategy in Marvel Legendary is building a tight deck around one hero class. Choose a class based on what is available in the HQ early. If you see multiple affordable Intelligence heroes, go Intelligence. Recruit aggressively into that class and KO starting cards whenever possible. By mid-game, you should be drawing 3-4 heroes of the same class in a single hand. Every Combo Power fires, generating massive Recruit or Attack. A well-built class engine can generate 10+ Attack in a turn, which is enough to fight the Mastermind.

walkthrough.title: Deck Archetype — The Attack Rush
walkthrough.text: Some games demand raw Attack as fast as possible — particularly against high-Attack Masterminds or Schemes that punish slow play. The Attack Rush focuses on heroes that generate Attack directly, ignoring Recruit-heavy heroes entirely. S.H.I.E.L.D. Troopers (your starting Attack cards) are actually useful here — do not KO them. Instead, supplement them with cheap Attack heroes. The goal is to fight villains early and often, clearing the city and accumulating Victory Points while other players build their engines. This strategy shines in 4-5 player games where someone needs to manage the city.

walkthrough.title: Deck Archetype — The Recruit Engine
walkthrough.text: The opposite approach: build a Recruit-heavy deck to snap up the most expensive heroes in the HQ. Heroes costing 6 or 7 Recruit tend to have the strongest abilities — often generating 3-4 Attack plus powerful Combo Powers. By recruiting these early, you build toward a late-game powerhouse that can fight the Mastermind repeatedly. The risk is that you contribute little to fighting villains early, so this works best when at least one other player is covering city management. In 2-player games, this strategy is risky because one player cannot hold the city alone.

walkthrough.title: Mastering the Mastermind Fight
walkthrough.text: Defeating the Mastermind is how you win the game, so plan for it. Each Mastermind has a specific Attack threshold — often 7 to 10+. Work backwards: to hit 8 Attack, you might need 3 heroes each contributing 2-3 Attack through base values and Combo Powers. Know when you are likely to draw a killer hand. If your deck has 5 strong Attack heroes and you draw 6 cards, odds are good you will get several together. Time your Mastermind fight for when you have recently shuffled your discard pile into your deck, maximizing the chance of drawing your best cards together.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Scheme
walkthrough.text: Different Schemes demand different strategies. A Scheme that says 'Evil Wins when 7 Scheme Twists occur' gives you a predictable countdown — plan your Mastermind fights accordingly. A Scheme that punishes escaping villains (like losing heroes from the HQ) demands aggressive city management. A Scheme that gains Wounds forces you to include KO and healing in your strategy. Before the game begins, read the Scheme thoroughly and discuss with your team how it changes priorities. The Scheme should dictate your overall approach more than any other factor.

walkthrough.title: Hero Evaluation — Beyond Raw Numbers
walkthrough.text: Experienced players evaluate heroes on four axes. First, efficiency: a cost-3 hero that gives 2 Attack is more efficient than a cost-6 hero that gives 3 Attack. Second, Combo Power quality: a Combo Power that gives +3 Attack is much better than one that gives +1 Recruit. Third, class synergy: does this hero match the class you are already collecting? Fourth, special abilities: heroes that draw extra cards, KO cards from your hand or deck, or reduce villain Attack are extremely powerful and often undervalued by new players. The best heroes score high on all four axes.

walkthrough.title: Cooperative Communication
walkthrough.text: Good communication wins games. Before each turn, quickly share your hand situation — 'I have about 4 Attack this turn' or 'I can recruit but cannot fight anything.' This helps the team coordinate. If a villain is about to escape, the player with the most Attack should prioritize fighting it. If the Mastermind is close to defeat, the player with the strongest combo potential should save their Attack for the Mastermind rather than spending it on a low-value villain. There is no rule against talking about your hand in Legendary — use it.

walkthrough.title: Adjusting Difficulty
walkthrough.text: Once you have mastered the basics, you can adjust difficulty in several ways. Mastermind Attack values vary significantly — higher Attack Masterminds are much harder to defeat 4 times. Some Schemes are more punishing than others, especially in combination with certain Mastermind or Villain Group pairings. Villain Groups themselves range from easy to brutal. For an easier game, pick a low-Attack Mastermind and a mild Scheme. For a challenge, combine a high-Attack Mastermind with a punishing Scheme and tough Villain Groups. Solo play has special rules: use 3 Heroes (42 cards), 1 Villain Group, 3 Henchman cards, 1 Bystander, and the normal Scheme Twists plus 5 Master Strikes.

walkthrough.title: Victory Point Optimization
walkthrough.text: When the team is on track to win, shift your focus to individual Victory Points. Mastermind Tactic cards are the highest-value targets (typically 5 VP each), so being the player who lands the Mastermind fight matters. Villains with captured Bystanders are also high-value targets. Late in the game, if you can choose between fighting a 1-VP villain and letting another player handle the city while you position for the Mastermind, the Mastermind is almost always worth more. But never prioritize VP over team survival — a loss gives everyone zero.

walkthrough.title: Wound Management
walkthrough.text: Wounds are more than a nuisance — they actively sabotage your deck. Drawing a Wound means you effectively have a 5-card hand instead of 6. Multiple Wounds compound the problem rapidly. Use the Healing ability when you have 2 or more Wounds in hand: skip recruiting and fighting for one turn to KO all Wounds from your hand. This is almost always worth it. Some heroes have abilities that KO Wounds directly — these cards are high-priority recruits when Wounds are piling up. A few heroes even benefit from Wounds, turning them into Attack or Recruit. These niche heroes can be game-changers against Masterminds that inflict heavy Wounds.

summary.title: Deck Archetype — The Class Engine
bullets: Build around 1 hero class for reliable Combo Power triggers
Recruit same-class heroes aggressively, KO starting cards
Mid-game: 3-4 same-class heroes per hand = massive output
Well-built engine can generate 10+ Attack for Mastermind fights

summary.title: Deck Archetype — The Attack Rush
bullets: Focus on Attack-generating heroes, ignore Recruit-heavy options
Keep S.H.I.E.L.D. Troopers — they contribute Attack early
Fight villains early and often to clear the city
Best in 4-5 player games where someone must manage threats

summary.title: Deck Archetype — The Recruit Engine
bullets: Build Recruit-heavy deck to buy the most expensive heroes (cost 6-7)
Late-game powerhouse with 3-4 Attack per hero + strong Combo Powers
Risk: poor early city contribution
Works best with 3+ players where others can fight early

summary.title: Mastering the Mastermind Fight
bullets: Work backwards from Mastermind's Attack threshold (often 7-10+)
Need 3+ heroes generating 2-3 Attack each via base + Combo Powers
Time your fight after a deck shuffle for best card density
Can fight Mastermind multiple times in one amazing turn

summary.title: Reading the Scheme
bullets: Scheme dictates overall strategy more than any other factor
Countdown Schemes: plan Mastermind fights around Twist count
Escape-punishing Schemes: aggressive city management required
Wound-heavy Schemes: prioritize KO and healing abilities

summary.title: Hero Evaluation — Beyond Raw Numbers
bullets: Efficiency: cost vs. output ratio (cost-3 for 2 Attack > cost-6 for 3)
Combo Power quality: +3 Attack combo >> +1 Recruit combo
Class synergy: matches your existing class focus
Special abilities: card draw, KO, villain Attack reduction often undervalued

summary.title: Cooperative Communication
bullets: Share hand strength: 'I have ~4 Attack' or 'I can only recruit this turn'
Coordinate who fights escaping villains vs. who recruits
Player with strongest combo should save Attack for Mastermind
No rule against discussing hands — communication wins games

summary.title: Adjusting Difficulty
bullets: Higher Mastermind Attack = harder game
Some Scheme + Mastermind combos are especially brutal
Villain Groups vary in difficulty
Solo play: 3 Heroes (42 cards), 1 Villain Group, 3 Henchman cards, 1 Bystander, 5 Master Strikes

summary.title: Victory Point Optimization
bullets: Mastermind Tactics = highest VP targets (~5 each)
Villains with Bystanders = high-value fights
Position for Mastermind fights when team is winning
Never prioritize VP over team survival

summary.title: Wound Management
bullets: Each Wound = effectively one fewer card in hand
Healing: skip recruiting/fighting to KO all Wounds from hand (worth it with 2+ Wounds)
Heroes that KO Wounds are high-priority recruits
Some heroes benefit from Wounds — niche but powerful

entries.definition: The main villain boss that all players must cooperate to defeat. Has an Attack value, a Master Strike effect, an 'Always Leads' Villain Group, and 4 Mastermind Tactic cards. Players win by defeating the Mastermind 4 times. Masterminds do NOT count as Villains for card effects.

One of 4 cards tucked under the Mastermind. When you fight the Mastermind, you draw one random Tactic. It goes to your Victory Pile (typically worth 5 VP) and its Fight effect triggers. All 4 share the same Attack value as the Mastermind but have different Fight effects.
The Mastermind's evil plan. Defines how many Scheme Twists go into the Villain Deck, special rules, and the 'Evil Wins' condition. If the Evil Wins condition is met, all players lose.

A card in the Villain Deck that triggers the Twist effect on the Scheme card when flipped. Advances the Mastermind's plan. Removed from play after resolving unless the Scheme says otherwise. Does not push villains forward in the city.
A card in the Villain Deck (always 5 in every game) that triggers the Master Strike effect on the Mastermind card. Removed from play after resolving. Does not push villains forward.

An enemy card that enters the city from the Villain Deck. Has an Attack value, a Villain Group affiliation, and may have Fight, Ambush, or Escape effects. Defeated villains go to the defeating player's Victory Pile.
A set of 8 unique Villain cards that share a theme, like 'Ernak the Lethal' or 'Brigitte Blackbird.' The Mastermind always leads one specific group. Additional groups are added based on player count.
A set of 10 identical weaker Villain cards added to the Villain Deck. Fewer abilities than full Villain Groups but still must be fought. Number used depends on player count.

A card representing a hero that can be recruited from the HQ. Has a cost (lower-right), Recruit Points, Attack, a hero class, a faction, and possibly a Combo Power or special ability. Heroes go to your discard pile when recruited.

An innocent civilian card. 30 in the Bystander stack. Some are shuffled into the Villain Deck during setup. When flipped, they are captured by the villain closest to the Villain Deck (or the Mastermind). Worth 1 Victory Point when rescued. Do not push villains forward.

A negative card forced into your discard pile by villain abilities. Has no Recruit or Attack. Drawing Wounds weakens your hand. Can be KO'd via the Healing ability (skip recruiting and fighting for the turn) or specific hero powers.
A starting hero card in every player's deck. Each player begins with 8 Agents. Provides 1 Recruit Point when played. Has no hero class (grey/basic). KO-ing these thins your deck.
A starting hero card in every player's deck. Each player begins with 4 Troopers. Provides 1 Attack when played. Has no hero class (grey/basic). KO-ing these thins your deck.

A basic hero always available for recruitment from the Officer stack (30 cards). Costs 3 Recruit to recruit. Provides 2 Recruit when played. Has no hero class. Better than Agents but weaker than named heroes.

The resource used to recruit heroes from the HQ or the S.H.I.E.L.D. Officer stack. Shown as a star icon on hero cards. Generated by playing cards during Step 2 of your turn. Does not carry over between turns.

The resource used to fight Villains in the city and the Mastermind. Shown as a claw/fist icon on hero cards. Generated by playing cards during Step 2. Does not carry over between turns. Must equal or exceed a villain's Attack value to defeat it.

Points earned by defeating Villains, claiming Mastermind Tactic cards, and rescuing Bystanders. Stored in your Victory Pile. The player with the most VP after the Mastermind is defeated wins individually.

The row of 5 spaces where Villains lurk. New villains enter at the space closest to the Villain Deck and push existing villains forward. A villain pushed past the 5th space escapes. Only one villain per city space.
The row of 5 face-up hero card spaces. Players recruit heroes from here by paying their Recruit cost. Empty spaces refill immediately from the Hero Deck.
The face-down deck of hero cards used to refill the HQ. Built from 5 randomly chosen Heroes (14 cards each = 70 total). With 5 players, a 6th Hero is added (84 total). Placed near the HQ.
The face-down deck flipped at the start of every turn. Contains Villain Groups, Henchman Groups, Bystanders, Scheme Twists, and Master Strikes. Built during setup based on player count and Scheme.
Each player's personal face-down pile of defeated Villains, claimed Mastermind Tactics, and rescued Bystanders. Cards here are never shuffled into your deck. VP is tallied from this pile at game end.
The shared pile for all permanently removed cards. KO'd cards are out of the game for good. Players and the game share one KO pile.
Each player's personal pile of used cards. Cards played during your turn and unplayed hand cards go here at end of turn. When your deck is empty, your discard pile is shuffled to form a new deck.

A conditional ability on a hero card that activates only if you have already played another card of the same hero class earlier this turn. Shown with a class icon and colon before the ability text. Triggers at most once per turn per card.
An effect on a Villain card that triggers the moment that villain enters the city from the Villain Deck. Resolve Escape effects of pushed villains before the new villain's Ambush.

An effect on a Villain card that triggers when that villain is pushed off the 5th city space. In addition to the Escape effect, the escaping villain always KOs a hero costing 6 or less from the HQ, and each player discards a card if the villain carried Bystanders.

An effect on a Villain or Mastermind Tactic card that triggers when it is defeated. Fight effects resolve before the villain goes to your Victory Pile. They can be beneficial or harmful depending on the card.

Remove a card permanently from the game into the shared KO pile. Can affect heroes in your hand, heroes you played this turn, or cards specified by abilities. KO-ing weak starting heroes is beneficial for deck thinning.

The ability printed on Wound cards: 'If you don't recruit any Heroes or defeat any Villains on your turn, you may KO all the Wounds from your hand.' You can still play cards and use non-recruit/non-fight abilities before healing.

Take the top Bystander from the Bystander stack and place it in your Victory Pile (worth 1 VP). Does NOT let you rescue Bystanders captured by specific Villains in the city — you must defeat those Villains.
When a card says a player 'gains' a card, that card goes into the player's discard pile. The player will draw it later when their discard pile is shuffled into a new deck.
Show a card to prove you have it. You do not play or discard the revealed card. Can reveal from your hand or from cards already played this turn.
One of the five hero classes. Represents heroes with raw physical power, determination, and strong leadership. Shown by the Strength icon in the card's upper left corner.
One of the five hero classes. Represents heroes who use knowledge, quick wits, wisdom, and instincts. Shown by the Intelligence icon in the card's upper left corner.
One of the five hero classes. Represents heroes trained in unique talents — weapons mastery, sleight of hand, and specialized abilities. Shown by the Skill icon in the card's upper left corner.
One of the five hero classes. Represents masters of wizardry, sorcery, and enchantment. They use spells and mystical items. Shown by the Magic icon in the card's upper left corner.
One of the five hero classes. Represents heroes who strike from a distance — bows, guns, wands, or supernatural abilities. Shown by the Ranged icon in the card's upper left corner.
The class of starting heroes (S.H.I.E.L.D. Agents, Troopers) and S.H.I.E.L.D. Officers. Grey is its own color for abilities that count hero colors. Basic heroes do not trigger Combo Powers.
The number of Recruit Points required to recruit a hero from the HQ, shown in the lower-right corner of the card. Ranges from 1 to 8+. S.H.I.E.L.D. Officers always cost 3.
The number shown on a Villain card representing how much Attack players need to defeat it. Ranges from 2 to 7+ for regular Villains. You must have Attack equal to or greater than this value.
The number on the Mastermind card (and shared by all its Tactic cards) representing how much Attack is needed to fight the Mastermind. Typically 7 to 10+. Higher values are harder games.
Text on the Mastermind card specifying which Villain Group must always be included in the Villain Deck when that Mastermind is in play.

The condition on the Scheme card that causes all players to lose immediately. Examples include a certain number of Scheme Twists occurring or villains escaping. Track this condition carefully throughout the game.

When your personal draw deck runs out and you need to draw or reveal cards, shuffle your discard pile to form a new deck. Only happens when the deck is completely empty. Your discard pile is never shuffled prematurely.
Players always draw 6 cards at the end of their turn. There is no way to permanently increase hand size. Some card abilities let you draw additional cards during your turn.
Refers to both cards in your hand AND cards you have played this turn. Does NOT include heroes in your deck or discard pile. Important for abilities like 'KO one of your Heroes.'

entries.term: Each Hero You Played This Turn
entries.definition: Only counts cards already played this turn, not cards still in your hand. Important for abilities that scale based on previously played heroes.

A grouping of heroes by allegiance, shown by an icon in the upper-left corner of hero cards. Some Combo Powers use faction icons instead of hero class icons. Works the same way — play two heroes of the same faction to trigger the combo.

All players win together when the Mastermind's 4 Tactic cards are all defeated. The individual winner is the player with the most Victory Points. If players defeat the Mastermind, everyone wins — but one wins the most.
All players lose if the Scheme's Evil Wins condition is met. The game ends immediately. No individual winner.

If the Hero Deck or Villain Deck runs out before the Scheme is completed, the game is a draw. Players survived but did not defeat the Mastermind. The player with the most Victory Points wins an individual victory.

Special rules for 1-player games. Use 3 Heroes (42 cards), ignore the Mastermind's 'Always Leads,' use 1 Villain Group, 3 Henchman cards from one group, 1 Bystander, 5 Master Strikes, and normal Scheme Twists. When a Scheme Twist resolves, put a hero costing 6 or less from HQ on bottom of Hero Deck. When a Master Strike resolves, play another Villain Deck card. 'Each other player' effects apply to you.

The game scales by adjusting Villain Deck composition. 2 players: 2 Villain Groups, 1 Henchman Group, 2 Bystanders. 3 players: 3 Villain Groups, 1 Henchman, 8 Bystanders. 4 players: 3 Villain Groups, 2 Henchman, 8 Bystanders. 5 players: 4 Villain Groups, 2 Henchman, 12 Bystanders, plus a 6th Hero in the Hero Deck.

Difficulty can be tuned by choosing Mastermind (higher Attack = harder), Scheme (some are more punishing), and Villain Groups (some are tougher). Combining a high-Attack Mastermind with a harsh Scheme and brutal Villain Groups creates the hardest experience.

In solo play, if you win, add up Victory Points and subtract penalties: -4 for each Bystander carried away by escaping Villains, -3 for each Scheme Twist that occurred, and -1 for each Villain that escaped. Compete against your own high scores.
